title: The Attack on DEI Was a Ruse. We Compared the Qualifications of Obama and Trump Administration Appointees. The Record Is Telling.
teaser: The argument against DEI programs has been consistent: they substitute identity for merit, producing less qualified people. That is a testable claim. We tested it. We pulled the academic credentials and professional records of cabinet-level appointees from two administrations, set them side by side, and let the documented record speak. It does.
verifiedSource: U.S. Senate Confirmation Records; Nobel Prize Committee Archives; EPA Official Biographies; Senate Judiciary Committee Transcripts; MIT Official Faculty Records; Texas A&M University Registrar; Princeton University Public Records; C-SPAN Congressional Archive
body:
This is not an opinion piece. It does not argue that one administration was morally superior to another. It does not use the word “racist.” It does not speculate about motive. It compares documented academic credentials and verifiable professional experience, department by department, appointee by appointee, across two presidential administrations. The evidence base is public record: Senate confirmation filings, official government biographies, university archives, and the C-SPAN footage that never lies.

The argument made in January 2025, encoded in executive orders eliminating federal DEI offices, was explicit: diversity, equity, and inclusion programs substitute identity characteristics for merit, and this substitution produces less qualified people in consequential roles. That is a testable hypothesis. We tested it.

The record is telling.

Department of Energy: The Nobel Laureate and the Man Who Forgot the Name

Barack Obama appointed Steven Chu as Secretary of Energy in 2009. Chu held a B.A. in Mathematics and a B.S. in Physics from the University of Rochester, and a Ph.D. in Physics from the University of California, Berkeley. He was a professor at both Stanford University and UC Berkeley. He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1997. He understood what the Department of Energy does because he had spent decades doing the work that the Department of Energy funds.

Obama's second Energy Secretary was Ernest Moniz, who held a B.A. from Boston College and a Ph.D. in Theoretical Physics from Stanford. He was a Professor of Physics at MIT and the founding director of the MIT Energy Initiative, one of the most significant academic energy policy programs in the country.

Donald Trump appointed Rick Perry as Secretary of Energy in 2017. Perry holds a B.S. in Animal Science from Texas A&M University. He served as Governor of Texas for fourteen years, a legitimate executive credential. He is also the man who, during his 2012 presidential campaign, publicly committed to eliminating three federal departments and then, on live television, could not recall the name of the third. The department he forgot was the Department of Energy. He was subsequently confirmed to lead it.

The administration that championed DEI produced a Nobel laureate and an MIT theoretical physicist to run the agency responsible for the United States nuclear weapons stockpile and energy research. The administration that declared DEI a threat to merit produced an animal science graduate who forgot the agency existed.

Department of Education: Professionals vs. the Brand Deal

Obama's first Education Secretary, Arne Duncan, held a B.A. in Sociology from Harvard University. He had served as CEO of Chicago Public Schools from 2001 to 2009, overseeing one of the largest urban school districts in the country. His second Education Secretary, John King, held a B.A. from Harvard, a J.D. from Yale Law School, and an Ed.D. from Columbia University. King had served as New York State Education Commissioner and had taught in public schools.

Trump's first Education Secretary was Betsy DeVos, who holds a B.A. in Business Administration and Political Science from Calvin University in Michigan. She had never attended a public school. She had never taught in a public school. She had never worked in a school administration. During her Senate confirmation hearing, she indicated she may have been confused about whether the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act, the federal law protecting students with disabilities, was a federal or state matter. The IDEA is a federal law. It has been federal law since 1975.

She was confirmed by a 51-50 vote, with Vice President Mike Pence casting the deciding vote. It remains the only time in United States history that a vice presidential tiebreaker was required to confirm a cabinet nominee.

Trump's 2025 Education Secretary was Linda McMahon, who attended East Carolina University without completing a degree. Her primary professional credential is serving as CEO of World Wrestling Entertainment. She previously served as Small Business Administrator during Trump's first term. She has no documented background in educational policy, curriculum, pedagogy, special education, or school administration.

Department of Housing and Urban Development: The Surgeon and the Housing Experts

Obama appointed Shaun Donovan to lead HUD in 2009. Donovan holds a B.A. and a Master of Architecture from Harvard University and a Master of Public Administration from Harvard's Kennedy School. He had served as Commissioner of the New York City Department of Housing Preservation and Development and had previously worked at HUD under the Clinton administration. He had spent his career in housing policy.

Obama's second HUD Secretary, Julian Castro, holds a B.A. from Stanford University and a J.D. from Harvard Law School. He served as Mayor of San Antonio, where housing development was a central policy priority, and had worked extensively on urban development issues throughout his career.

Trump appointed Ben Carson to lead HUD. Carson holds a B.A. from Yale University and an M.D. from the University of Michigan Medical School. He is a gifted surgeon whose professional contributions in pediatric neurosurgery are extensively documented and genuine. He had no prior experience in housing policy. He had no background in urban development. He had written in his 2015 book A More Perfect Union that subsidized housing creates what he called a form of modern slavery, a claim he made without citation to housing research. He was confirmed to lead the agency responsible for fair housing enforcement, public housing administration, and federal mortgage programs.

Environmental Protection Agency: The Litigant Leads the Agency He Sued

Obama's first EPA Administrator, Lisa Jackson, holds a B.S. in Chemical Engineering from Tulane University and an M.S. in Chemical Engineering from Princeton. She had worked at the EPA for sixteen years before her appointment, developing the expertise in environmental regulation that the job requires.

His second EPA Administrator, Gina McCarthy, holds a B.A. in Social Anthropology from the University of Massachusetts Boston and an M.S. in Environmental Health Engineering from Tufts University. She had worked in environmental policy for thirty-five years. She had served five Republican governors in Massachusetts before her federal appointment.

Trump appointed Scott Pruitt as EPA Administrator in 2017. Pruitt holds a B.A. from Georgetown College and a J.D. from the University of Tulsa College of Law. As Attorney General of Oklahoma, he had joined or led fourteen separate lawsuits against the Environmental Protection Agency. He had been, by documented legal record, one of the most active litigants against the agency he was nominated to lead. He resigned in July 2018 amid a reported fourteen separate federal ethics investigations.

Department of Health and Human Services: Public Health Credentials vs. Vaccine Court Records

Obama appointed Kathleen Sebelius to lead HHS. Sebelius holds a B.A. from Trinity Washington University and a Master of Public Administration from the University of Kansas. She had served as Kansas Insurance Commissioner and as Governor of Kansas, positions that required direct engagement with healthcare markets, Medicaid, and public health infrastructure.

His second HHS Secretary, Sylvia Mathews Burwell, holds a B.A. from Harvard University and an M.Phil. in International Relations from Oxford University. She served as President of the Global Development Program at the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation, where she managed one of the largest global health funding portfolios in existence, before serving as Director of the Office of Management and Budget.

Trump's 2025 HHS Secretary is Robert F. Kennedy Jr. Kennedy holds a B.A. from Harvard University and a J.D. from the University of Virginia School of Law. He is an environmental lawyer by training and practice. He holds no medical degree. He holds no degree in public health, epidemiology, or any related field. He has no documented experience administering a health system, managing a public health agency, or developing health policy. He is the founder of Children's Health Defense, which federal courts and public health researchers have documented as a primary institutional source of vaccine misinformation. He now leads the agency responsible for the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, the Food and Drug Administration, the National Institutes of Health, and Medicare and Medicaid.

Attorney General: From Harvard Law to a Nomination That Could Not Survive

Eric Holder, Obama's first Attorney General, holds an A.B. from Columbia University and a J.D. from Columbia Law School. He served as a federal prosecutor in the Public Integrity Section of the Department of Justice, as a judge on the Superior Court of the District of Columbia, and as Deputy Attorney General of the United States under President Clinton. He spent decades inside the institution before leading it.

Loretta Lynch, Obama's second Attorney General, holds an A.B. from Harvard College and a J.D. from Harvard Law School. She served twice as United States Attorney for the Eastern District of New York, one of the most complex federal prosecutorial districts in the country, handling cases involving international organized crime, terrorism financing, and public corruption.

Trump's 2025 nomination for Attorney General was Matt Gaetz, who holds a B.S. from the College of William and Mary and a J.D. from Florida State University. He served three terms in the House of Representatives. He had never prosecuted a case. He had never served as a state or federal attorney general. He had never led a law enforcement agency at any level. His nomination was withdrawn before a Senate confirmation hearing could be scheduled.

What the Pattern Shows

The pattern across these comparisons is not a matter of party preference. It is a matter of documented record. The Obama administration, repeatedly characterized in 2025 as a DEI administration whose diversity commitments came at the expense of merit, consistently appointed people with deep subject-matter expertise to the agencies they led. A Nobel Prize-winning physicist to Energy. A career housing policy administrator to HUD. A sixteen-year EPA veteran to the EPA. Harvard Law prosecutors to the Justice Department.

The Trump administration, which made the elimination of DEI a stated first-day priority on the grounds that DEI produces unqualified people, appointed an animal science graduate to Energy, a woman who had never attended a public school to Education, a surgeon with no housing policy background to HUD, the lead litigant against the EPA to lead the EPA, and a lawyer with no public health credentials to lead the public health apparatus of the United States government.

This is not a subjective interpretation. These are the credentials. They are in the Senate confirmation records. They are in the university archives. They are on C-SPAN.

The Question the Record Raises

If the attack on DEI were actually about qualifications, the documented record would support it. It does not. The people removed from federal service in January 2025 included career civil servants with advanced degrees in the fields they worked in, professionals with decades of subject-matter experience, and agency heads who had spent their lives in the work they were asked to do.

The people appointed to replace institutional expertise with political alignment have, in multiple documented cases, less formal education in relevant fields, less professional experience in the agencies they lead, and in at least one case, a track record of active legal opposition to the agency's core mission.

The attack on DEI was not, the record suggests, primarily about qualifications. The documented comparison makes that clear. What it was primarily about is a question this article does not answer. The record only shows what it was not about.

It was not about qualifications.
